title: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
author: Guest Writer
orientation: A new abolitionist (Tim Martin) testifies that the work of abolishing abortion must be rooted first in prayer and repentance, recounting how watching Sen. Dusty Deevers and attending the DC mission exposed his own apathy and self-righteousness. The piece is a pastoral exhortation: pray with faith, defend Christ with gentleness, and trust God to abolish abortion to His glory.
audiences: New abolitionist examining their own heart
Pastor or church leader discipling abolitionists
Pro-life Christian skeptical that prayer is 'doing' anything

questions.question: Why does the author claim the abolition movement will fail without prayer?
questions.audience: New abolitionist examining their own heart
questions.answer: Because abolition is God's work before it is man's work. Without entrusting the labor to God, activists will exhaust themselves in vain effort, and the movement itself will collapse under the weight of unaided human striving.
questions.quote: If the abolition movement does not begin with prayer, it will die. This may seem a hyperbolic statement, but unless the builders entrust the work of abolition to God, we will certainly labor in vain.

questions.question: What three things does the author say abolitionist prayer must include?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader discipling abolitionists
questions.answer: Repentance of apathy toward the slaughter of the preborn, supplication that God would give us sorrow for the lost, and remembrance that the labor exists to represent and glorify Christ rather than to vindicate ourselves.
questions.quote: This prayer needs to be, firstly, one of repentance of apathy, supplication to cause us to be sorrowful for the lost, and remembrance that we represent and glorify Christ in this labor.

questions.question: How did watching Sen. Dusty Deevers convict the author about his own abolitionist convictions?
questions.answer: Deevers' tender, near-sobbing care for the preborn during the SB-456 hearing exposed that the author's own abolitionism had been merely intellectual rather than a faith-shaped position that allowed God's word to expose his apathy.
questions.quote: For me, holding to the abolitionist viewpoint beforehand was simply intellectual; it was not a position I had taken in faith, allowing for the Lord to correct me, a sinner amid a holocaust, who was trying to point other sinners to their inconsistency.

questions.question: What sinful posture did the author discover in himself toward those involved in abortion?
questions.answer: He realized he had been treating himself as morally superior to people directly complicit in abortion, while ignoring that only God's restraining grace had kept him from the same sin. He also lacked sorrow for the unsaved souls of mothers who have killed their children.
questions.quote: I was stunned to realize I thought of myself as above those that were directly involved in the slaughter but denied God’s grace that restrained me from being involved myself. I also lacked sorrow for those lost souls who have murdered their children and are not covered by Christ.

questions.question: Where does the author say the fight against abortion actually begins?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian skeptical that prayer is 'doing' anything
questions.answer: Not on the streets or at the capitol, but on one's knees before a holy God who hates the death of the innocent and detests human apathy toward it. The starting line is repentance.
questions.quote: The fight against abortion does not begin on the streets; rather, it starts on your knees before a holy God that abominates the death of the innocent and abhors the apathy of man. It begins with repentance for ignoring such a horrific act.

questions.question: What was the author's biggest regret from the Washington, DC mission?
questions.answer: That he prayed too little and talked too much, defaulting to self-justification in front of those he was supposedly trying to win, rather than graciously pursuing their reconciliation with God.
questions.quote: My greatest regret from the DC mission was that I prayed little and said much.

questions.question: Which line from 1 Peter 3:15 does the author say apologists most often forget, and why does it matter?
questions.answer: The closing phrase 'yet do it with gentleness and respect.' Forgetting it tempts Christians to defend Christ in a way that contradicts Christ's own character, attacking image-bearers instead of loving them as God loved us.
questions.quote: The last line of 1 Peter 3:15, which is frequently used in apologetics — “yet do it with gentleness and respect” — is the most forgotten aspect of the passage. It demonstrates how easily humans are tempted to defend Christ “Christlessly.”

questions.question: What does the author promise will happen when abolitionists pray faithfully?
questions.audience: New abolitionist examining their own heart
questions.answer: God will plant seeds that supernaturally turn darkness into light, and abortion will be abolished for His glory. The believer's confidence rests not in the strategy but in Christ, who sanctifies and protects His bride.
questions.quote: When we faithfully pray, God plants seeds that will supernaturally transform darkness into light, and, likewise, abortion **will** be abolished to the glory of God.

related.title: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
related.reason: Companion Guest Writer testimony from the same Washington, DC mission, illustrating the gentleness-and-respect posture this article calls for.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Another Guest Writer piece making the parallel argument that intellectual assent to abolitionism is hollow without genuine repentance.

related.title: Abolitionists Must Stand Firm to Oppose Murder, Love Murderers
related.reason: Develops the same tension the author confesses: hating the slaughter of the preborn while loving the mothers who have killed them.

related.title: Norman Statement
related.reason: The doctrinal statement the author identifies as a signer of in his bio, naming the theological commitments behind his exhortation to prayer.

related.title: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
related.reason: Provides the broader doctrinal frame for treating abolition as a labor that must be 'entrusted to God' rather than waged in human strength.

related.title: Biblical, Not Secular
related.reason: Reinforces the article's premise that abolition is a Scripture-rooted obedience, not a pragmatic political project.

related.title: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
related.reason: A pastoral call to repentance that mirrors this article's claim that the abolitionist's first work is on his knees.
